# Questions in Livestream 112

Live Stream #112: Questions Are The Reward

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=486" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:06</a> | Was Mordecai made a eunuch when he entered Ahasuerus' service in Esther 8:9? | He said no; Mordecai was given a one-off commission to write a decree in the king's name, not made a permanent palace official. | He read Esther 8:7-10 as the king authorizing Esther and Mordecai to write a counter-decree in the king's name and seal it with the royal signet. Mordecai was commanding the content of that specific decree after Haman's downfall, not entering a standing palace role that would imply being made a eunuch. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=887" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:47</a> | How widespread were royal signet rings in the Persian world? | He said they were tightly controlled, with essentially one true royal signet typically handled by a seal-bearer rather than widely dispersed. | He described the signet as an instrument of delegated royal authority, normally in the physical custody of a seal-bearer or comparable official rather than scattered among many officials. The seal-bearer could travel with soldiers to authorize documents for the king, reducing the risk that the ring would be stolen and misused. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=966" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:06</a> | Were Persian gallows a crucifixion rather than a noose? | He said no; Persian gallows meant impalement on a sharpened pole rather than hanging by the neck. | He clarified that the Persian gallows in this context were neither a modern noose nor a crucifixion. They were an apparatus with a sharpened pole for impalement, so the death was not by neck-suffocation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=1061" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:41</a> | How did Hatshepsut prove military skill as pharaoh? | He said she largely did not; she stayed out of campaigns and delegated war-making to generals. | He said Hatshepsut was unusual because most Egyptian kings proved physical prowess through war and punitive campaigns, but she did not personally go to war. When campaigns were needed, she delegated them to generals, making her reign distinctive and leaving few recorded military campaigns associated directly with her. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=1206" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:06</a> | Are people forbidden from developing biotechnology that could make humans immortal? | He distinguished life extension from immortality and said making humans unable to die would be extremely unwise even if not stated as a simple prohibition. | He distinguished legitimate life-extending technologies from making humans incapable of dying. He connected the idea to Revelation 9:6 and to Genesis' Tree of Life narrative, arguing that God prevents unregenerate immortality, so pursuing absolute biological immortality would be tempting judgment even if not phrased as a direct biblical ban. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=1475" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:35</a> | What was the state of the Egyptian military under Thutmose III? | He described it as highly trained, well equipped, and arguably the most capable army Egypt ever fielded. | He rejected the idea that looting proved inexperience, saying immediate looting was standard behavior for ancient armies. Thutmose III was raised in military life, was popular with the army, took a hands-on role, and fielded a force so capable that he stopped at Kadesh because he had achieved his aims, not because the army could go no farther. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=1612" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:52</a> | In Judges 1:16, why did Moses' in-laws go from Jericho, the city of palms, if Joshua 6:26 cursed anyone rebuilding Jericho? | He said the city of palms was a small village, not the rebuilt fortified Jericho; the curse is fulfilled later in 1 Kings 16:34. | He distinguished a small agricultural village at the viable water source from a formal rebuilding of fortified Jericho. He then pointed to 1 Kings 16:34, in Ahab's time, as the text that records the curse being fulfilled when Jericho was rebuilt with foundations and gates. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=1940" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:20</a> | If Merneptah attacked Israel in the 1100s, could that be a misspeaking of the century? | He said it was probably a misspeaking, but still thought the broader historical argument needed work. | He treated the date phrasing as likely an accidental century mistake, but said the underlying Merneptah argument still matters because the Merneptah Stele places Israel in Canaan before some late Exodus reconstructions allow. He remained critical of attempts to compress the conquest and settlement evidence into an 1100s framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=2221" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:01</a> | What does he think of alternative theories for the origin of Yahweh? | He rejected the moon-god theory and said he finds a Midian-linked origin more plausible than the view that explicit Yahweh worship was already present in Avaris. | He dismissed the moon-god proposal as unpersuasive and treated a southern or Midianite connection as more plausible. He allowed that other divine names may have been used by related groups, but he did not think explicit Yahweh worship at Avaris had been demonstrated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=2431" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:31</a> | Did Zoroastrianism influence early Judaism? | He said not the earliest forms; later prophetic or postexilic material may show limited Persian influence, but he thinks the influence is often overstated. | He separated early Israelite religion from later Jewish material under Persian rule. In his view, some later angelology, demonology, or postexilic framing may reflect Persian-period contact, but claims that Zoroastrianism shaped Judaism from the beginning are overstated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=2579" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:59</a> | Does Irenaeus' statement about the church being caught up support a pre-tribulation rapture? | He said no; he read it as premillennial rather than pre-tribulational and called the pre-trib reading anachronistic. | He said Irenaeus can be read as expecting a future catching up of the church, but not as teaching the modern pre-tribulation rapture scheme. He framed the passage as premillennial in broad eschatology and warned against projecting later dispensational categories back into Irenaeus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=2777" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:17</a> | Is Jabin in Judges 4 the same Jabin as in Joshua 11? | He said no, and suggested the later `Hazor` title may reflect dynastic or relocated continuity rather than the same ruler. | He rejected identifying the two Jabins as the same individual because the narratives are separated historically. He suggested Jabin may have functioned as a dynastic or royal title connected with Hazor, or that a later ruler claimed continuity with the earlier Hazor tradition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=3009" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:09</a> | Why did Egyptians give Ramesses III a hard time economically even though he helped defend Egypt from the Sea Peoples? | He said rampant inflation and loss of imperial revenue damaged daily life, so military success did not preserve popularity decades later. | He said Ramesses III's early victories over the Sea Peoples did not protect him from later economic collapse. As Egypt lost control of Nubia and perhaps much of Canaan, imperial revenue no longer cushioned inflation, prices rose, families could not afford grain, and unrest eventually reached the level of assassination conspiracy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=3229" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:49</a> | Were the Sea Peoples one people or several, and were they Cretan or Greek? | He said they were several Indo-European groups, likely tied to the Greek archipelago and western Anatolia, including groups later associated with the Philistines. | He described the Sea Peoples as multiple Indo-European tribes rather than a single ethnicity. He mentioned groups such as the Danuna, Sherden, Peleset, and Lukka, locating their movement from the Greek archipelago and western Anatolia down the Levantine coast before being stopped near the Sinai. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=3405" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:45</a> | Why do some scholars place the Exodus during the reign of Ramesses III? | He said they usually do so from theories about late Pentateuchal or Hebrew development rather than from the Egyptian archaeological evidence itself. | He said scholars such as Manfred Bietak and Gary Rendsburg arrive at a Ramesses III Exodus mainly through late-dating Hebrew language or Pentateuchal development, not Egyptian evidence. He objected that the Merneptah Stele, Israelite settlement evidence by about 1150, and Late Bronze destruction patterns make that reconstruction difficult. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=3643" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:43</a> | What are his thoughts on Genesis 4:26, and was Yahweh worshiped in other cultures besides the patriarchs and Hebrews? | He read the verse as retrojective language about calling on God, and said some southern Levantine groups may have worshiped Yahweh or related divine names. | He treated Genesis as a text that gives Israel a cohesive shared history, so he read "calling on the name of the Lord" as retrojective language for calling on God rather than proof that early humans literally knew the name Yahweh. He allowed that Midianites, perhaps Shasu or Jebusite-related groups, may have worshiped Yahweh or related names such as El Elyon or El Shaddai. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=3889" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:49</a> | Which Egyptian archaeological dig interests him most now, and which biblically linked dig interests him most? | He mentioned Pithom, Wadi Tumilat, Heliopolis, and Hazor as especially interesting because of their potential biblical or background relevance. | He singled out Pithom and Wadi Tumilat because of finds tied to the Exodus background, including an inscription from Pithom. He also mentioned Heliopolis because of Genesis connections to On and Asenath, and Hazor because finding a scriptorium there would illuminate biblical background culture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=4072" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:52</a> | What is his most controversial theological position, stated provocatively? | He said his Exodus date is his most controversial position and has cost him academic opportunities. | He said the date of the Exodus is the position that has caused him the most professional controversy. He described losing teaching opportunities or interviews over saying anything at all about it, and said he had a separate video explaining why the topic is so controversial. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=4185" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:45</a> | When the Israelites left Egypt, would they have used the Egyptian calendar, and do calendar differences make biblical dates harder to pinpoint? | He said the early Hebrew calendar was Canaanite rather than Egyptian, later calendars reflected hegemony, and changing systems can complicate date calculations. | He said the later Hebrew calendar reflects Babylonian influence, but early Israel used a Canaanite or Gezer-style calendar for practical reasons such as trade and local contracts. He noted that Israel knew Egyptian calendar features, including thirty-day civil months, but used lunar and observational months, which can make long chronological calculations difficult. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=4463" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:23</a> | How does the evidence suggest Akhenaten died? | He said the evidence is unclear, but the unfinished burial goods suggest a sudden death and an awkward transition. | He said there is no direct record of how Akhenaten died, only that he reigned about fifteen to seventeen years and probably died suddenly. The transition itself was peaceful, but unfinished or repurposed burial equipment later found with Tutankhamun suggests Akhenaten's elaborate burial project was incomplete at death and inherited by successors. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=4690" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:10</a> | Is he going to ETS this year? | He said yes. | He answered directly that he was going to ETS that year and noted that the questioner might bump into him there. No further qualification was added beyond confirming his attendance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=4722" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:42</a> | Does he see Jewish roots to the sacraments? | He said yes, especially communion in Passover and baptism in Jewish ritual washing and initiation practices. | He grounded communion in the Passover meal, with Jesus identifying the bread and cup with his body and blood as the Lamb of God. For baptism, he connected Christian practice to Jewish ritual washing, mikveh practice, Second Temple initiation customs, and John the Baptist's use of those meanings for covenant initiation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=4925" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:05</a> | Did some Second Temple Jews believe they were eating the same Passover as Moses and everyone else? | He said he had not seen that in the texts and would defer to a specialist in Second Temple Judaism. | He said he had not seen anything like a transubstantiation-style claim in the Jewish texts he knows, but also emphasized that Second Temple Judaism is not his specialty. He treated the question as one that would need a specialist for an authoritative answer and mostly passed on it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=5090" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:50</a> | How much autonomy did Egyptian scribes have in what they wrote? | He said very little. Most scribes were bureaucratic clerks and copyists doing assigned work rather than exercising academic freedom. | He described scribes as clerks and bureaucrats doing piecework: copying texts, producing administrative documents, calculating grain, writing letters, recording temple spells, and accompanying armies to count casualties. Their writing served the bureaucracy, temple, king, or paying client rather than personal academic freedom. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=5222" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:02</a> | Why did Thutmose III deface only Hatshepsut's public monuments rather than private material too? | He said the goal was to destroy her public image, so private or tomb contexts were less relevant and sometimes sacrosanct. | He said private tomb contexts could be treated as sacrosanct even when someone was hated, so there was no reason to desecrate every private image. The defacement worked representatively by attacking public reputation and afterlife image, comparable to other Egyptian desecration campaigns that targeted names and images without being exhaustive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=5380" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:40</a> | Was Moses' Cushite wife in Numbers 12 Zipporah or someone else, and why was Miriam punished? | He said she was someone else; the issue involved tribal alliance and prophetic authority, and Miriam was punished for entering the tent of meeting. | He said Zipporah was Midianite, so the Cushite wife was a different woman. He framed Miriam and Aaron's objection as a tribal-alliance and authority dispute, not a race issue: they challenged Moses' claim to hear God, entered the tent of meeting, and Miriam was struck because that sacred space was restricted. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=5676" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:36</a> | Did ancient Egyptians have riots before the first recorded workers' strike? | He said there are no records of earlier riots, though food insecurity could have produced unrest before the first attested strike. | He said Dynasty 20 gives the first recorded workers' strike, and he knew of no earlier riot records. He allowed that unrest could have happened in sequestered villages when food supply failed, since riots usually form around food insecurity, but that remains inference rather than documented evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=5743" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:43</a> | Were the prophecies against Tyre fulfilled even though Alexander, not Nebuchadnezzar, is often associated with Tyre's destruction? | He said there is evidence Nebuchadnezzar did attack or control Tyre, and Alexander can be part of the broader many-nations fulfillment. | He cited two cuneiform tablets, one about preparations to invade Tyre and another treating Tyre as a Babylonian province, as evidence that Nebuchadnezzar likely did attack and control the city. Because the prophecy also speaks of many nations, Alexander's later attack can be part of the larger series rather than replacing Nebuchadnezzar. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=5869" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:49</a> | Why was there democratization of afterlife material in Egypt, with texts spreading beyond royalty? | He said the process began earlier than the Second Intermediate Period, from late Old Kingdom and Middle Kingdom developments such as mastabas and coffin texts. | He said democratization of the afterlife began well before the Book of the Dead became widespread. Courtiers and nobles began building mastabas in Dynasty 6, coffin texts adapted pyramid-text traditions for non-royal elites in the Middle Kingdom, and ideas such as the ba and ka expanded beyond the king over time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=6054" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:54</a> | Where does he think Adam and Eve lived? | He guessed southern Mesopotamia because of the rivers and regional markers in Eden traditions, while noting exact location is difficult. | He located Eden generally in Mesopotamia because Genesis names rivers such as the Tigris and Euphrates and refers to regional markers like Kish. He added that sea-level rise and delta changes may have submerged or altered the relevant southern Mesopotamian landscape, so the exact location remains uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=6174" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:54</a> | Did the Hebrews have a base-ten system, and what about other ancient Near Eastern cultures? | He said Israelites and Egyptians used base ten, while Mesopotamians also used base sixty for mathematics and timekeeping. | He said Israelites counted in tens, hundreds, and thousands, and Egyptians likewise used base ten. Mesopotamians used both base ten and base sixty, with base sixty especially useful for mathematics, geometry, and the timekeeping system that later gives sixty minutes on a clock. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=6302" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:02</a> | Are we living in the end times? | He said the New Testament treats the last days as beginning with the resurrection, but no one can identify the final consummation in advance. | He answered yes in the broad New Testament sense that the last days begin with Christ's resurrection. But he distinguished that from the final consummation, saying no one knows the day or hour, the return of Christ will be obvious rather than hidden, and confident date-setters should not be trusted. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=6483" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:03</a> | Which Egyptian social class did the most innovative work, such as improving chariots? | He said innovation depended on the field; Hyksos-related groups adapted chariots, while physicians, scribes, engineers, and craftsmen innovated in different areas. | He said the first chariot adaptation came through Amorite or Hyksos-related groups, then was customized for Egyptian warfare. Innovation was not confined to one class: physicians experimented medically, scribes and planners solved construction and hydrology problems, and craftsmen handled technical improvements in their own specialties. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=6704" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:44</a> | What kinds of books would have been held in Egyptian libraries around 1600 BC and later? | He said Egypt had scriptoria rather than public libraries, with contents depending on temple, palace, administrative, or religious function. | He said true public libraries come later with the Greeks, while earlier Egypt had restricted scriptoria. Temple scriptoria would hold liturgies, spells, and religious texts; palace or administrative scriptoria would hold correspondence, contracts, and records; access depended on the institution and the user's status. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=6884" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:44</a> | In Revelation 14:14, does Jesus' sickle have an Old Testament connotation? | He said it is primarily an agricultural harvest metaphor, connected to the wheat and tares imagery. | He read Revelation 14's sickle as a harvesting image: the earth is ripe, the wheat and tares are gathered, and separation comes through the harvest process. He connected it especially to Jesus' parable in Matthew 13 rather than treating the sickle mainly as a separate Old Testament symbol. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=7134" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:54</a> | What would he say to an atheist who says Christianity cannot be believed because the Bible was written by men long ago and changed over time? | He said age alone is not an argument against truth, and textual criticism shows the biblical message has remained remarkably stable despite minor changes. | He separated the objection into two claims. "It is old" is just an appeal to age and does not make a belief false; "it changed" is more serious, but writing functions as a mnemonic device, textual criticism can identify variants, and the overall biblical message remains unusually stable compared with other ancient texts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=7424" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:44</a> | What are his thoughts on C. S. Lewis' The Great Divorce? | He said he had not read it and could not comment. | He said he had read some C. S. Lewis, including Mere Christianity and The Screwtape Letters, but not The Great Divorce. Because he did not know the book well enough, he explicitly passed on evaluating it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=7537" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:37</a> | Which political system is promoted as optimal in the Torah and Jewish sources? | He said Torah establishes a theocratic center for Israel but does not present monarchy or the judgeship as an optimal universal political model. | He said Torah gives Israel a way to live in the promised land around a theocratic center, but it does not promote a universal best form of government. Monarchy is not treated as ideal, the judges fail, and the biblical material does not speak directly to an optimal system for all peoples. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=7754" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:14</a> | Why does the Israelite calendar seem to have more Babylonian than Egyptian influence? | He said calendar usage followed hegemony and exile, with later biblical collation reflecting Mesopotamian influence while Egyptian influence appears in some periods. | He said the books of Kings and Chronicles were collated after the Babylonian exile, so later sections naturally show Babylonian and Assyrian calendar influence. Egyptian calendar influence also appears in some contexts, such as after Josiah's overthrow by Necho II, but the dominant later framing follows the hegemonic context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=7893" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:33</a> | Are there theological differences between David and Kiara? | He said Kiara leans more Arminian while he leans more Calvinist. | He gave a brief personal answer: the major theological difference is that Kiara leans more toward Arminianism while he leans more toward Calvinism. He did not elaborate beyond that contrast in this answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=7963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:43</a> | If there was no death before the fall, what was the purpose of the Tree of Life? | He said there was likely some kind of death before the fall, and the Tree of Life would have made human immortality permanent. | He argued that some kind of death existed before the fall, such as leaves withering or food dying in digestion, but the key issue is human mortality. The Tree of Life would have confirmed or made permanent human immortality, and he speculated that Adam and Eve's problem was taking the knowledge of good and evil out of order. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=8174" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:14</a> | Why do many believe mankind existed before Adam and Eve, and could Eve from Adam's rib be metaphorical? | He said views differ widely, often depending on evolution and age-of-earth assumptions, and he did not take a firm position. | He said some interpreters distinguish pre-Adam biological humans from Adam as a special creation with moral and spiritual responsibility, often depending on evolution and age-of-earth assumptions. On Eve's creation from Adam's rib, he said interpreters range from metaphorical to literal or mythological readings, and he did not offer a firm personal conclusion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=8349" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:09</a> | How could business be conducted in Egypt without pharaonic involvement if Pharaoh sponsored major projects? | He said ordinary business operated through barter and private contracting; royal sponsorship was mainly for major royal or temple projects. | He described Egypt as a barter economy where people exchanged goods and services until both sides were satisfied. Pharaoh sponsored major projects such as temples, royal tombs, and state work, but private nobles still contracted craftsmen for their own tombs and paid for ordinary economic activity themselves. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=8493" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:33</a> | Why was Sobekneferu hailed as a savior of Egypt when Egypt declined after her reign? | He said this was putting a brave face on a declining dynasty, especially in religious or official inscriptions. | He said the Twelfth Dynasty was already failing, Sobekneferu was the only viable ruler, and official language tried to keep the bureaucracy and public from panicking. Religious inscriptions especially avoid bad news, so praising her as a savior reflects institutional optimism and propaganda more than proof that the dynasty was healthy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=8714" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:14</a> | Why does Egyptian art not use perspective, and why do temples seem lacking in ornamentation? | He said perspective was difficult in carved stone though sometimes attempted, and temples were originally highly painted and decorated. | He said perspective was hard to execute in carved stone, though Egyptians did know of it and sometimes attempted it in paintings or plaster contexts. Temples only look plain now because paint is lost; sites such as Medinet Habu and Dendera preserve enough color to show that reliefs were originally bright and heavily ornamented. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=8881" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:01</a> | Does he have a favorite clear fulfilled prophecy written before the event? | He named Isaiah's prophecy of Cyrus, while cautioning that most prophecy is not primarily future prediction. | He chose Isaiah's prophecy naming Cyrus, arguing that Cyrus was not yet the later famous ruler and that there are good reasons to treat the material as earlier than the event. He also cautioned that most prophecy is not prediction of distant events but clarification of the times in which the hearers live. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=9138" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:18</a> | What does he think of Neoplatonism and its influence on Christian doctrine? | He said it influenced some background and church fathers, but more strongly fed Gnosticism than Christianity, and he does not think it works well theologically. | He allowed some Platonic or Pythagorean background around New Testament language such as Logos, and noted that some church fathers were influenced by Neoplatonism. But he thought Neoplatonism shaped Gnosticism more strongly than orthodox Christianity and said its theological scheme does not work well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=9326" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:26</a> | Did inequality between men and women in Egypt increase after Ramesses II? | He said he would frame it more as changing sponsorship and cultic emphasis than a simple decline in women's status. | He emphasized that Egyptian women had many rights and that female-linked cults such as Mut's remained important. Changes in titles such as God's Wife of Amun reflect where royal and cultic sponsorship was directed at different times, not a straightforward decline in women's status. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=9885" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:45</a> | Why did rationalism or skepticism emerge in Middle Kingdom Egypt and then disappear by the 1100s? | He rejected the framing, saying the texts are better understood as religious idealization or speculation rather than atheistic rationalism. | He said Egyptian poets sometimes idealized religion or spoke of God in the singular, somewhat like later philosophical idealization, but that is not skepticism or atheism. Egyptians could speculate about the afterlife while still assuming the gods were self-evidently real, so he summarized the material as idealistic rather than rationalistic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=10178" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:38</a> | Did evil exist before the fall if the serpent was already trying to lead Adam away from God? | He said evil begins with the serpent's iniquity and the fall narrative, not before it. | He appealed to Ezekiel's picture of the serpent or covering cherub as perfect until iniquity was found in him. In his reading, evil begins with the serpent setting his mind toward rebellion and then with the fall of Adam, Eve, and the serpent in Genesis 3. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=10361" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:41</a> | Does Yahweh's name have to do with inhale and exhale? | He rejected that as an etymological argument with no merit, saying the name is tied to the verb "to be." | He dismissed the inhale-exhale claim as a baseless etymology. He said the origin of Yahweh is tied to a form or tense of the verb "to be" and treated that as the serious linguistic explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=10529" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:29</a> | Does Nicholas of Cusa rightly imply that Adam blamed God for introducing impurity into Eden through Eve? | He agreed Adam shifted blame onto both God and the woman, though he did not think that changed the judgment. | He agreed that Adam's response shifts blame to both God and the woman, describing Adam as spreading blame once caught. He still said Eve was deceived and Adam was disobedient, and he did not think the blame-shifting would have changed Adam's judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=10721" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:41</a> | What was the most philosophical text in Egyptian literature, and how was such literature distributed? | He pointed to the Book of Thoth and related teacher-student traditions, saying such works were copied and distributed on papyri. | He named the Book of Thoth, especially in the trajectory later associated with Hermes Trismegistus traditions, and also mentioned philosophical material in Ramesside Leiden papyri. He stressed that even this literature has a religious bent and that such works circulated on copied papyri rather than public walls. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=10947" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:27</a> | How many hours was the Egyptian work day, and was the average work day hard? | He said it was usually around ten hours, varied by job, and could be hard and dangerous for farmers or builders but easier for scribes. | He said the work day followed daylight, roughly from morning to evening, and usually lasted about ten hours. Difficulty depended on occupation: scribes had relatively comfortable desk work, farmers and pyramid builders worked hard and sometimes dangerously, and the Egyptian ten-day work week gave only a half day off on the tenth day. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=11102" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:02</a> | When early Exodus advocates say to trust calendar years, which calendar do they mean? | He said they usually mean a Julian-style solar year, which he considers an anachronistic standard for biblical interpretation. | He said early Exodus advocates who count years this way usually assume a 365.25-day Julian-style solar year. In his view, that imports a modern scientific calendar standard into biblical chronology and assumes prophets counted years by a later anachronistic measure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=11204" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:44</a> | What suggestions does he have for those struggling with sin? | He recommended prayer, Bible reading, regular confession to God, short accounts, and accountability where needed. | He recommended keeping communication open with God through prayer, reading Scripture for spiritual strength, and confessing sins quickly and regularly. He described salvation as an amnesty in which God wants agreement that sin is sin and a desire to change, and he said accountability groups can help with persistent struggles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=11467" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:07</a> | What are his thoughts on women pastors and elders? | He said the issue is complicated; he sees women pastors as appropriate in some ministries, but treats eldership and spiritual oversight as a distinct responsibility. | He said women already do much of the actual ministry work in churches and that women pastors can be appropriate in certain ministries. He distinguished that from eldership or spiritual oversight, which he framed not as an honor but as the heavy responsibility to feed, guide, correct, rebuke, and reprove the flock. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=12023" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:23</a> | How does Jesus reveal himself to people on the fence of faith or to non-believers? | He recommended reading the Gospels and Scripture, saying the Holy Spirit works through the text to form deep roots. | He advised people on the fence to start with the Gospels and good biblical teaching so they learn who Jesus is. He said the Holy Spirit enlightens Scripture and speaks through the God-breathed text, so deep roots in faith should begin with sustained Bible reading rather than waiting years to develop them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=12277" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:37</a> | If evolution turned out to be true, would it conflict with humanity being made in the image of God? | He said no; the image of God concerns moral and spiritual capacity rather than biological mechanism. | He said even if evolution were true, it would not negate divine intervention or the image of God. Humanity is defined by moral choice, the living soul, and spiritual capacity, not merely biology, so an evolutionary mechanism would be secondary to God's act of giving humans moral and spiritual personhood. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=12766" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:46</a> | In light of Heiser's Deuteronomy 32:8 model about Yahweh versus the gods, did the first engagement with the gods start with Egypt? | He said he does not agree with that model for Deuteronomy 32:8, seeing it as a Second Temple reading applied to Bronze Age literature. | He said the question is almost a nonstarter for him because he does not accept Heiser's Deuteronomy 32:8 model. He thinks the passage is better about division of lands than nations being handed to divine council gods, and he sees Heiser's reading as applying Second Temple categories to Bronze Age material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=13038" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:18</a> | Why does he lean toward Calvinism, and does Calvinism deny free will? | He said he leans toward total depravity but is not a five-point Calvinist, and that some Calvinist views allow free will while denying unaided movement toward God. | He said Calvinism depends on which version is meant: some forms are fatalistic, but moderate versions affirm human freedom to sin while denying that people can come to Christ unaided. He personally finds total depravity persuasive, is not a five-point Calvinist, and described himself as leaning more Calvinist than Arminian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=13151" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:11</a> | Which verses would he use to refute a Jehovah's Witness? | He recommended Isaiah 44:6 because he reads it as presenting both the king of Israel and his Redeemer as Jehovah while affirming one God. | He said Isaiah 44:6 is useful because, when "Lord" is read as Jehovah in Jehovah's Witness terms, the passage distinguishes Jehovah the king of Israel from his Redeemer, Jehovah of hosts, while still saying there is no God besides him. He uses that to argue that the text presents more than one person under the divine name, not Jesus as a created being. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=13450" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:44:10</a> | If the Solomonic temple is similar to many pagan temples, does that mean God can dwell in something similar to a pagan temple? | He said shared ancient Near Eastern temple forms do not matter because their meaning is redeemed and redirected to Yahweh worship. | He said ancient Near Eastern rituals and temple forms shared common cultural vocabulary, including sacred and profane space. In Israel, the meaning is inverted and redeemed for Yahweh worship: symbols like cherubim and processional furniture are used to show that God has no idol, does not need human care, and remains enthroned without being fed or clothed by priests. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=13722" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:48:42</a> | How does he feel about Zahi Hawass' contributions to archaeology and Egyptology? | He said Hawass is a legitimate scholar, while also noting his political connectedness and telling a story about avoiding direct criticism. | He affirmed Hawass as a legitimate scholar with academic work, but stressed that criticizing someone so politically connected can be career-limiting. He illustrated that caution with an anecdote about a conference and the risk of asking Hawass a provocative Exodus question in public. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MlFIL9cvfsg?t=13895" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:35</a> | When did Egypt's supposedly incestuous royal marriage practice end? | He said the premise is overstated; incestuous marriages were rare, often misread from kinship terms of endearment, and royal cases ended with the monarchy. | He said the broad claim of frequent Egyptian incest is a misunderstanding caused partly by spouses calling each other brother and sister as terms of endearment. He cited a study of 400 marriages with only two consanguineous cases, treated royal examples as rare succession or power-sharing exceptions, and said the practice ended for royalty when Egypt no longer had native royalty. |
